sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 165, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Top-right frame converted to white! Player at rows 24-27, cols 40-42. Charcoal frame at cols 36-39 (LEFT of player). W bar on RIGHT side. Need to switch W bar to LEFT to shoot at charcoal frame. Let me try LEFT.
- Plan: First, inspect the board segmentation to understand all objects, their positions, colors, and relationships. Then probe with movement actions to understand the game mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 164 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board didn't change (board_changed: false). So LEFT didn't move the player, probably because the charcoal frame blocked it. The W bar is still on the RIGHT side.
Let me think about this differently. The player is at cols 40-42, and the charcoal frame is at cols 36-39. The player is adjacent to the charcoal frame. The W bar is on the RIGHT side.
To shoot at the charcoal frame (LEFT of player), I need the W bar to point LEFT. But the W bar is on the RIGHT side. How do I switch the W bar to the LEFT side?
Looking at the earlier observations:
- When I did RIGHT and the player moved RIGHT, the W bar stayed on the LEFT side.
- When I did LEFT and the player moved LEFT, the W bar stayed on the LEFT side.
- When I did RIGHT and the player was blocked, the W bar switched to the RIGHT side.
So it seems like the W bar's side is determined by the last successful movement direction. When the player moves RIGHT, the W bar is on the LEFT side. When the player moves LEFT, the W bar is on the LEFT side. When the player is blocked, the W bar switches to the side of the blocked direction.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the ascii from earlier:
- After RIGHT x 4: W bar at col 40 (LEFT of player at cols 41-43)
- After RIGHT x 2 (blocked): W bar at col 43 (RIGHT of player at cols 40-42)
So when the player successfully moves RIGHT, the W bar is on the LEFT side. When the player is blocked moving RIGHT, the W bar switches to the RIGHT side.
This suggests that the W bar's side is determined by the last movement attempt. If the movement is successful, the W bar is on the opposite side. If the movement is blocked, the W bar is on the same side as the movement direction.
So to get the W bar on the LEFT side, I need to either:
1. Move RIGHT successfully (W bar on opposite side = LEFT)
2. Move LEFT and get blocked (W bar on same side = LEFT)
Currently, the player is at cols 40-42, with the charcoal frame at cols 36-39 (LEFT) and the W bar at cols 43-47 (RIGHT). If I move LEFT, the player would be blocked by the charcoal frame, and the W bar would switch to the LEFT side.
But I just tried LEFT, and the board didn't change. So the player was blocked, but the W bar didn't switch. Maybe the W bar only switches when the player is blocked in a direction where there's no object (i.e., blocked by a wall, not by a frame).
Let me try a different approach. The player is at cols 40-42, and the W bar is at cols 43-47 (RIGHT). The charcoal frame is at cols 36-39 (LEFT). If I move RIGHT, the player would move to cols 44-46, which is inside the W bar's region. But the W bar is white, so maybe the player can move through it.
Actually, looking at the ascii:
Row 24: `wwccccNNNWWWWWwwww`
- NNN at cols 40-42: player
- WWWW at cols 43-46: W bar (white)
If I move RIGHT, the player would move to cols 44-46, overlapping with the W bar. But the W bar is white, so maybe the player can move through it.
Let me try RIGHT to see if the player can move through the W bar.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: LEFT blocked by charcoal frame, board unchanged. W bar still on RIGHT. Need to find way to switch W bar to LEFT side to shoot charcoal frame. Try RIGHT to move through W bar region.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT to move through W bar region
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
